title: Forms
subtitle: Template-driven and Reactive Forms — handle user input the Angular way
description: Master both Angular form approaches — Template-driven for simple cases and Reactive Forms for complex validation, dynamic fields, and full programmatic control.
keyPoints: Template-driven forms use FormsModule and [(ngModel)] — easy for simple forms, hard to unit test
Reactive forms use ReactiveFormsModule, FormControl, FormGroup, and FormBuilder — fully programmatic and testable
FormBuilder.group() shorthand is the standard way to build reactive forms concisely
Validators.compose() and custom validator functions give fine-grained validation control
Cross-field validators go on the FormGroup, not individual controls
FormArray handles dynamic lists of fields (add/remove rows at runtime)
Async validators check uniqueness or server-side rules — they return Observable<ValidationErrors | null>

sections.title: Two Approaches — Template-Driven vs Reactive
sections.content:
Angular provides two completely different ways to build forms:

Template-Driven Forms:
• Driven by directives in the HTML template (ngModel, ngForm)
• Logic lives in the template — less TypeScript, more HTML
• Angular creates the FormControl objects automatically behind the scenes
• Good for: simple forms, quick prototypes, minimal validation
• Bad for: complex validation, dynamic fields, unit testing, large forms

Reactive Forms:
• Driven by TypeScript code — the form model lives in the component class
• Angular syncs the HTML to the model (not the other way around)
• You have full control over the form structure, validation, and state
• Good for: everything — complex validation, dynamic fields, testing, async validators
• Preferred in production Angular apps

Both approaches use the same underlying FormControl, FormGroup, and FormArray classes internally. Reactive forms just give you direct access to them.

sections.caption: Choose Reactive Forms for anything beyond a simple contact form. The extra setup pays off immediately with better testability, validation control, and dynamic form capabilities.

sections.title: Template-Driven Forms — ngModel and ngForm
sections.content:
Template-driven forms are the simpler approach. You add directives to your HTML and Angular handles the model creation automatically.

Key directives:
• ngForm — applied automatically to <form> elements when FormsModule is imported. Gives you access to the form's validity and values.
• ngModel — creates a FormControl for the field and binds it to a component property with two-way binding [(ngModel)].
• ngModelGroup — groups related controls into a nested FormGroup.
• required, minlength, maxlength, pattern, email — built-in HTML validators that Angular reads.

Accessing state with template reference variables:
• #myForm="ngForm" — gives you a reference to the NgForm directive
• #email="ngModel" — gives you a reference to the NgModel directive for a specific field
• These references expose .valid, .invalid, .touched, .dirty, .errors

sections.title: Template-Driven Form
sections.explanation: Always add the name attribute to every form field — without it, ngModel cannot register the control with the parent ngForm. Use #fieldName="ngModel" template references to access validation state per field without any TypeScript code.

sections.title: Reactive Forms — FormControl, FormGroup, FormBuilder
sections.content:
Reactive forms give you a programmatic model of the form in your TypeScript class. The model is the source of truth — the template binds to it.

Core building blocks:

FormControl — a single form field. Holds: the current value, validation status (valid/invalid/pending), touch/dirty state, and validation errors.

FormGroup — a collection of FormControls (or nested FormGroups). Valid only when ALL children are valid. Its value is an object with control names as keys.

FormArray — a dynamic list of FormControls or FormGroups. You can add and remove controls at runtime. Its value is an array.

FormBuilder — an injectable service that provides shorthand methods to create form groups without the verbose new FormControl() syntax:
• fb.group({ name: ['', Validators.required] })
• fb.control('initialValue')
• fb.array([])

sections.title: Reactive Form with FormBuilder and Validators
sections.explanation: Cross-field validators (like password matching) belong on the FormGroup, not on individual controls. The validator receives the entire group as AbstractControl, so it can read sibling control values. The error is then checked on the group: form.get('passwords')?.errors?.['mismatch'].

propagates up the tree</text></svg>
sections.caption: A FormGroup is VALID only when every child FormControl and nested FormGroup is valid. One invalid control makes the entire tree invalid. This is why form.invalid is a reliable submit guard.

sections.title: Built-in Validators and Custom Validators
sections.content:
Angular ships with built-in validators for common rules:
• Validators.required — field must not be empty
• Validators.minLength(n) — minimum n characters
• Validators.maxLength(n) — maximum n characters
• Validators.email — must match email pattern
• Validators.pattern(regex) — must match a regular expression
• Validators.min(n) — numeric minimum value
• Validators.max(n) — numeric maximum value
• Validators.requiredTrue — checkbox must be checked (terms & conditions)
• Validators.nullValidator — always returns null (no error)

Custom validators are plain functions that take an AbstractControl and return either null (valid) or an object (error). The error object key becomes the error name you check in the template.

Validators can also be methods on the component class, or standalone exported functions that can be shared across forms.

sections.title: Custom Sync and Async Validators
sections.explanation: Async validators receive a third argument position in FormBuilder.group() (after the sync validators array). While pending, control.status === 'PENDING' and control.pending === true — use this to show a spinner. Angular automatically waits for all async validators to resolve before setting the final validity status.

sections.title: FormArray — Dynamic Form Fields
sections.content:
FormArray is a collection of controls whose length can change at runtime. It is the correct tool for dynamic forms where users can add or remove items — addresses, phone numbers, tags, line items, skills.

FormArray key methods:
• .push(control) — add a control to the end
• .removeAt(index) — remove the control at the given index
• .at(index) — get the control at a specific index
• .clear() — remove all controls
• .controls — the array of controls (use in @for loops)
• .length — number of controls

In the template, bind a wrapping element to [formArrayName]="arrayName" and then use the array index as the formGroupName for each row.

sections.title: FormArray — Dynamic Skills List
sections.explanation: The FormArray index is used as the formGroupName in the template — [formGroupName]="$index". This is how Angular knows which FormGroup in the array each row is bound to. Always use formArrayName on the wrapper div and formGroupName on each row div.

sections.title: Form State — touched, dirty, pristine, pending
sections.content:
Every FormControl (and FormGroup) tracks five state flags:

• valid / invalid — whether all validators pass
• pending — async validator is currently running
• touched / untouched — whether the field has been focused and blurred (left)
• dirty / pristine — whether the value has been changed from its initial value
• enabled / disabled — whether the control participates in validation

Why these matter:
• Show validation errors only when touched — prevents error messages appearing immediately on page load before the user has interacted
• Warn user about unsaved changes by checking form.dirty before navigation
• Disable a field programmatically with control.disable() — disabled controls are excluded from form.value
• Use control.valueChanges observable to react to changes for conditional logic

Useful methods:
• form.markAllAsTouched() — mark every control as touched (show all errors on submit attempt)
• form.reset() — reset values to initial and clear touched/dirty state
• form.patchValue({ name: 'Ada' }) — update specific fields without affecting others
• form.setValue({ name: 'Ada', email: '...' }) — update ALL fields (must provide every field)

sections.title: Form State and Dynamic Control Manipulation
sections.explanation: Use valueChanges on a control to reactively add or remove validators on other controls based on user choices. Always call updateValueAndValidity() after changing validators dynamically — Angular does not re-run validation automatically when you call setValidators() or clearValidators().

sections.content: Disable the submit button with [disabled]="form.invalid" but also call form.markAllAsTouched() inside onSubmit() before the validity check. This ensures that if the user somehow bypasses the disabled button (e.g. presses Enter), all error messages are shown. markAllAsTouched() is the correct 'show me all errors now' call.

setValue() requires you to pass a value for EVERY control in the group. If you omit even one field, Angular throws an error. Use patchValue() when you want to update only some fields — it ignores any controls not included in the object you pass. patchValue() is almost always the right choice when loading data.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between Template-driven and Reactive Forms in Angular?
interviewQuestions.a: Template-driven forms use FormsModule and directives like ngModel in the HTML template. Angular automatically creates the form model behind the scenes. They are simpler but harder to test and limited for complex validation. Reactive forms use ReactiveFormsModule and you explicitly create FormControl/FormGroup/FormArray objects in TypeScript. The model is the source of truth and the template binds to it. Reactive forms are fully testable, support dynamic fields, complex validation, async validators, and are the recommended approach for production apps.

interviewQuestions.q: What is FormBuilder and why should you use it?
interviewQuestions.a: FormBuilder is an injectable service that provides shorthand factory methods (group(), control(), array()) for creating reactive form structures. Without it, you write: new FormGroup({ name: new FormControl('', Validators.required) }). With FormBuilder, you write: fb.group({ name: ['', Validators.required] }). The array notation [initialValue, syncValidators, asyncValidators] is much more concise, especially for large forms with many fields. FormBuilder does not change how forms work — it is purely a convenience wrapper.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you validate that two password fields match in a reactive form?
interviewQuestions.a: Use a cross-field validator on the FormGroup that contains both password controls, not on the individual controls. Pass the validator as the second argument to fb.group(): fb.group({ password: [...], confirm: [...] }, { validators: passwordMatchValidator }). The validator function receives the FormGroup as AbstractControl. Read both values with group.get('password')?.value and group.get('confirm')?.value. Return null if they match or { mismatch: true } if they don't. In the template, check the error on the group: form.get('passwords')?.errors?.['mismatch'].

interviewQuestions.q: What is a FormArray and when do you use it?
interviewQuestions.a: FormArray is a collection of form controls whose size can change at runtime. Use it when the form has a repeatable section — a list of addresses, skills, phone numbers, or invoice line items where users can add and remove rows. Key methods: push(control) to add, removeAt(index) to remove, at(index) to access, and controls to iterate. In the template, bind the container with formArrayName="skills" and each row with [formGroupName]="$index" for a nested FormGroup array.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you create an async validator? What state is the control in while it runs?
interviewQuestions.a: An async validator is a function that takes AbstractControl and returns Observable<ValidationErrors | null> (or a Promise). You pass it as the third argument to FormControl or the third position in FormBuilder array notation. While the async validator is running, control.status === 'PENDING' and control.pending === true — use this to show a loading spinner. Angular debouncing tip: wrap the HTTP call in timer(400).pipe(switchMap(() => http.get(...))) inside the validator to avoid an API call on every keystroke. Always catch errors and return of(null) to treat API failures as valid.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between touched, dirty, and pristine?
interviewQuestions.a: touched/untouched: whether the field has been focused and then blurred (left). Starts untouched. Set to touched when the user clicks a field and then clicks away. Use this to show validation errors only after the user has interacted with a field. dirty/pristine: whether the value has changed from its initial value. Starts pristine. Set to dirty when the user types anything. Use form.dirty to warn users about unsaved changes before navigation. Use [disabled]="form.pristine" on a Save button to disable it until something changes.

interviewQuestions.q: What does markAllAsTouched() do and when should you call it?
interviewQuestions.a: markAllAsTouched() sets every control in the form (and all nested FormGroups and FormArrays) to touched: true. You should call it at the start of your submit handler: if (form.invalid) { form.markAllAsTouched(); return; }. Without this, validation error messages only show for fields the user has personally interacted with. Calling markAllAsTouched() forces all error messages to display simultaneously, which is the standard UX pattern when a user clicks Submit on an incomplete form.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between patchValue() and setValue()?
interviewQuestions.a: setValue() requires you to provide a value for EVERY control in the FormGroup — missing even one throws a runtime error. patchValue() is lenient — you provide only the fields you want to update and Angular ignores the rest. Use patchValue() when loading data from an API, especially if the API response might not include every form field. Use setValue() only when you are certain you are providing complete data for the entire form structure and want Angular to catch any missing fields as an error.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you add or remove validators dynamically at runtime?
interviewQuestions.a: Call setValidators(validators) to set new validators (replaces all existing ones) or addValidators(validator) to add without replacing. Call clearValidators() to remove all validators. After any of these calls, you MUST call updateValueAndValidity() on the control to trigger re-evaluation — Angular does not automatically rerun validation when you change validators. Common pattern: in the valueChanges subscription of a checkbox, conditionally call setValidators(Validators.required) or clearValidators() on a dependent field, then call updateValueAndValidity().

interviewQuestions.q: How do you disable a form control and what happens to its value?
interviewQuestions.a: Call control.disable() to disable programmatically, or use [attr.disabled] or the disabled property binding in the template. A disabled control: is excluded from form.value (it does not appear in the value object), is excluded from validation (it is always considered valid), and has its CSS class updated (ng-disabled). To include disabled control values, use form.getRawValue() which returns values for all controls including disabled ones. Use control.enable() to re-enable.
